explanation_md: The `using Base::secret;` declaration in the `public:` section of `Derived` promotes `secret` from protected to public. Calling `d.secret()` is legal and calls the base's function, printing "Base secret". The `hello` function is demoted to private and would be a compile error to call on `d`.

prompt_md: A derived class demotes a base-class public function to private using a `using` declaration. Can that function still be called on a `Derived` object through a `Base&` reference?

options.a: No ŌĆö private means unreachable from anywhere
options.b: Yes ŌĆö access is checked using the static type of the expression, which is `Base&`
options.c: Only if the function is also `virtual`
options.d: Yes, but only from within the derived class body

explanation_md: C++ checks access control at compile time using the *static type* of the expression. When the variable is declared as `Base&`, the compiler sees `Base::function` which is public in `Base`, so the call is allowed. The derived class's private label only applies when the expression's static type is the derived type.

options.a: `ColorPrinter` cannot inherit from `Printer` because they have different `print` signatures
options.b: Defining `print(double)` in `ColorPrinter` hides all inherited `print` overloads; `cp.print()` has no match
options.c: `print()` is ambiguous because both `Printer::print()` and `ColorPrinter::print(double)` could match
options.d: `print(int)` and `print(double)` are ambiguous overloads that the compiler cannot resolve

explanation_md: Defining any function in a derived class with the same name as a base function hides all inherited overloads of that name ŌĆö even overloads with different parameter lists. Adding `using Printer::print;` to `ColorPrinter` restores all the inherited `print` overloads while keeping `print(double)` as well.

prompt_md: What is the difference between making an inherited function `private` in the derived class (via `using`) versus deleting it with `= delete`?

options.a: There is no practical difference ŌĆö both produce a compile error
options.b: `private` allows calls from within the derived class and from friends; `= delete` prohibits the function entirely including from within the class
options.c: `= delete` only works on virtual functions
options.d: `private` is a compile error; `= delete` produces a runtime error

explanation_md: A `private` member can still be called by the class's own member functions and friends. `= delete` makes the function unconditionally uncallable ŌĆö even from within the class body or from a friend. Use `= delete` when the operation must be truly prohibited, not just hidden from external users.
